dependencies.notes: payments-service cannot process transactions without Stripe and payments-db. Kafka failure degrades event publishing but does not block payments. bank-api is only used for nightly settlement — daytime failures are not immediately customer-facing. IMPORTANT: This service is PCI-DSS scoped. Any shell access to payments pods must be logged and is subject to audit.

common_issues.title: Payments timing out / checkout hanging
symptoms: Stripe dashboard shows no incoming requests (suggests we're not reaching Stripe)
Circuit breaker metric in Datadog showing OPEN state
Customer reports card charged but no order confirmation
likely_causes: Stripe API is degraded — check status.stripe.com first before doing anything else
Circuit breaker opened due to repeated failures and is now rejecting all requests
payments-service pods in CrashLoopBackOff due to DB or secret issue
Payments-db unreachable or pool exhausted

steps.command: curl -s https://www.stripestatus.com/api/v2/status.json | jq .status.description
steps.note: If Stripe is degraded, this is likely their fault. Monitor their incident. Our circuit breaker will auto-reset when Stripe recovers. Do not restart pods for a Stripe outage.

steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/status | jq .
steps.note: State should be CLOSED (healthy). OPEN means it's blocking all requests. HALF_OPEN means it's testing recovery. If OPEN, check why it opened — look at recent error logs.

steps.command: kubectl get pods -l app=payments-service
steps.note: Any CrashLoopBackOff pods are a problem. If you see this, do NOT restart — first check what the crash reason is.

steps.command: kubectl logs -l app=payments-service --previous | tail -50
steps.note: Look for DB connection errors, secret errors, or SSL errors. 'cannot connect to postgres' after a deploy usually means DB credentials are wrong in the k8s secret.

steps.action: Verify DB credentials secret is correct
steps.command: kubectl get secret payments-db-credentials -o json | jq '.data | map_values(@base64d)'
steps.note: Check that the host points to PRODUCTION postgres, not staging. This has happened before (INC-4021). sslmode must be 'require'.

steps.action: If Stripe is fine and pods are healthy, manually reset circuit breaker
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- curl -s -X POST localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/reset
steps.note: Only do this if you have confirmed the upstream (Stripe) is healthy. Resetting while Stripe is still degraded will just cause it to open again immediately.

common_issues.escalation: Any payment processing outage lasting more than 5 minutes is a P1. Page payments on-call and notify #payments-eng. If customers were charged but orders not created, that is also a P1 — involve customer support lead.

common_issues.title: Duplicate charges or double processing
symptoms: payments-db shows two transaction records for same order
Stripe dashboard shows duplicate payment intents for same customer
likely_causes: Idempotency key not being set on Stripe API calls (or being generated incorrectly)
Webhook retry from Stripe being processed multiple times
Frontend retrying payment after timeout without idempotency token

steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT order_id, count(*) FROM transactions GROUP BY order_id HAVING count(*) > 1 LIMIT 10;"
steps.note: Identify affected order_ids. Get exact timestamps of both charges to understand the sequence.

steps.action: Check Stripe dashboard for duplicate payment intents
steps.command: Log into Stripe dashboard, search by customer email or order ID. Look for multiple PaymentIntents with identical metadata.
steps.note: If both charges succeeded, you need to refund one immediately. Do not wait.

steps.action: Issue manual refund for duplicate charge via Stripe dashboard
steps.command: Stripe dashboard > Payments > find duplicate charge > Refund
steps.note: Document the refund amount and reason in the incident ticket. Notify customer support to reach out to affected customer.

steps.action: Check if idempotency key logic is functioning
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/payments-service | grep 'idempotency_key' | tail -20
steps.note: Idempotency key should be set on every Stripe call. If absent from logs, this is a code bug. Pull the relevant PR and check.

steps.command: Log into Stripe dashboard > Developers > Webhooks > check retry attempts for recent events
steps.note: If Stripe retried a webhook 3 times and we processed each one, our idempotency check on webhooks isn't working. Check payments-service code for webhook deduplication logic.

common_issues.escalation: Any confirmed double-charge is a financial incident. Notify bob@example.com and the CFO's team. Do not communicate specifics to customers until legal/finance has reviewed.

common_issues.title: Specific card type failing (e.g., Amex)
symptoms: Subset of users with specific card type getting payment errors
Stripe logs show requests going to wrong endpoint
likely_causes: Card-type routing hardcoded to wrong Stripe endpoint (this happened in v3.17.0 — Amex requires /v1/payment_intents, Visa/MC uses /v1/charges)
Stripe account not enabled for specific card type
Currency / country restriction on card type

steps.action: Identify which card types are failing
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/payments-service | grep -i 'error\|fail' | grep -i 'card\|amex\|visa' | tail -30
steps.note: Stripe error messages usually contain card brand info. Identify the pattern.

steps.action: Check which Stripe endpoint is being called for failing card type
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/payments-service | grep 'stripe request' | grep -i '<card_type>'
steps.note: Amex MUST go to /v1/payment_intents. Visa/MC can use /v1/charges. If Amex is going to /v1/charges, that's your bug. This is a code issue not a config issue.

steps.command: kubectl get deploy/payments-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image'
steps.note: If on v3.17.0, this is a known bug. Roll back to v3.15.2 or forward to v3.18.0+.

steps.action: Rollback if on known bad version
steps.command: kubectl rollout undo deployment/payments-service
steps.note: Verify with kubectl rollout status deployment/payments-service. Check payments work after rollback before closing incident.

steps.command: Query payments-db for failed transactions in the affected window. Cross-reference with Stripe for error codes.
steps.note: Customers whose Amex was declined may have tried other cards. Check for duplicate successful charges too.

common_issues.escalation: Card-type failures are P2 unless the failure is total (all cards failing). Notify #payments-eng. If financial data integrity is at risk, escalate to P1.

common_issues.title: Refunds being processed incorrectly (wrong amount or duplicated)
symptoms: Customer received refund of incorrect amount
likely_causes: Dollar-to-cents conversion applied twice (amounts stored in cents internally, refund API may accept dollars — check API contract)
Stripe webhook retries causing refund handler to run multiple times
Missing idempotency check on refund endpoint

steps.action: Stop the bleeding — disable refund endpoint if actively misfiring
steps.command: kubectl set env deployment/payments-service REFUND_ENDPOINT_ENABLED=false
steps.note: Only do this if refunds are actively processing incorrectly and you can't fix immediately. Communicate to support team that refunds are temporarily paused.

steps.action: Check refund amounts in payments-db vs Stripe
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT id, order_id, amount_cents, refund_amount_cents, created_at FROM refunds ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;"
steps.note: Compare refund_amount_cents against what's in Stripe dashboard. A 100x discrepancy suggests unit conversion bug.

steps.command: Look at the refund handler in payments-service source. Check if amount is converted to cents before being passed to Stripe — Stripe expects amounts in the smallest currency unit (cents for USD).
steps.note: This isn't something you can check at runtime. Pull the code. If you see the value being divided by 100 AND multiplied by 100, that's the bug.

steps.command: For overpaid refunds, contact Stripe support. They can sometimes claw back excess amounts if reported quickly.
steps.note: Realistically, if the money has been returned to the customer's card, recovery is very difficult. Finance and legal need to decide how to handle this.

common_issues.escalation: Any financial integrity issue is escalated to bob@example.com and finance team immediately. Do not attempt to fix refund errors without a second engineer reviewing.

common_issues.title: payments-db write failures / connection pool exhausted
symptoms: Logs show 'connection pool exhausted' or 'too many connections'
Transactions failing at persist step despite Stripe succeeding
likely_causes: Connection pool size too large relative to Postgres max_connections
Connections not being returned to pool after use (leak)
Too many payments-service pods each holding large connection pools
Long-running transactions holding connections

steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE datname = 'payments';"
steps.note: Postgres max_connections is currently 200. We should not be above 150 under normal load. If above 180, something is leaking.

steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT pid, state, query_start, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE datname='payments' ORDER BY query_start ASC LIMIT 20;"
steps.note: Idle connections older than 10 minutes are suspicious. idle in transaction is particularly bad — means a transaction was opened and not committed or rolled back.

steps.action: If connections are stuck, terminate the worst offenders
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'idle in transaction' AND query_start < NOW() - INTERVAL '5 minutes';"
steps.note: Do this carefully. Terminating active transactions will cause in-flight payments to fail and possibly need reprocessing. Get a second opinion first.

steps.command: kubectl get deploy/payments-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env[] | select(.name | startswith("DB_"))'
steps.note: Pool size × pod count should be less than 70% of Postgres max_connections. If you have 5 pods each with pool size 50, that's 250 connections — above the limit.

steps.action: Short-term fix: scale down payments-service pods to reduce connection count
steps.command: kubectl scale deployment/payments-service --replicas=2
steps.note: This reduces capacity but reduces connections proportionally. Only do this during off-peak hours. Remember to scale back up.

common_issues.escalation: DB connection exhaustion on payments is P1 — write failures mean lost transactions. Page payments on-call and optionally DBA team.

common_issues.title: Nightly bank settlement failing (bank-api errors)
symptoms: Morning alert: 'settlement job failed' in PagerDuty
bank-api returning 4xx or 5xx in nightly job logs
likely_causes: bank-api credentials expired or rotated without updating k8s secret
Settlement file format rejected (ClearBank occasionally updates their schema without warning)
Network connectivity issue between eu-west-1 and ClearBank endpoints
Payments being processed outside allowed settlement window

steps.command: kubectl logs job/nightly-settlement --since=12h | tail -100
steps.note: Look for the specific error from bank-api. 401 = credentials issue. 400 = format issue. 503 = their problem.

steps.command: kubectl get secret bank-api-credentials -o json | jq '.data.api_key | @base64d' | head -c 20
steps.note: Only print the first 20 chars for verification. Compare with what's in the password vault. ClearBank credentials rotate every 90 days — check if rotation is overdue.

steps.action: Check ClearBank status / contact their support
steps.command: Check their status page or email your ClearBank TAM. Their support email is in the vendor contacts doc (link in Confluence — ask Bob if you can't find it).
steps.note: ClearBank doesn't have a public status page. Their SLA response time is 4 hours during business hours (UK time).

steps.action: Manually retry settlement job if safe to do so
steps.command: kubectl create job --from=cronjob/nightly-settlement manual-settlement-$(date +%Y%m%d)
steps.note: Only retry if you've confirmed the job is idempotent for today's run. Check if any partial settlement was sent — double-settling could cause overpayment to merchants.

common_issues.escalation: Failed settlement affects merchant payouts. If settlement has not completed by 09:00 UK time, escalate to Bob and notify merchant success team.

general_health_checks: kubectl get pods -l app=payments-service — all pods Running, no restarts in last hour
Check Datadog: 'Payments Overview' dashboard — payment success rate should be >99.5%
Check Stripe dashboard for any unusual error rate spikes
Check payments-db connection count: should be < 150 under normal load
known_gaps: No automated test for Amex card routing in CI — added manually after INC-4021 but coverage is incomplete
Refund endpoint has no admin UI — all refunds via psql or Stripe dashboard
bank-api credential rotation procedure is manual and not well documented — ask Bob
Settlement job retry logic is not idempotent in all edge cases — verify before manual retry
PCI audit log archival to S3 not verified end-to-end since last audit
notes: This service is PCI-DSS scoped. All access is logged. Do not print full card numbers or full API keys to logs or Slack under any circumstances. If you suspect a security breach involving payment data, follow the security incident response plan (separate doc — find it, seriously, find it before you need it).
